explanation: The requirement mandates that the transition from Self-Test Mode to VentilationOff Mode (StandbyMode) should only occur when either the self-test procedure has successfully completed (selfTestPass) or the GUI requests resumption of ventilation (guiResumeRequest). In LTL, we want to capture that whenever the system is in SelfTestMode, a transition to StandbyMode (i.e., SelfTestMode becomes false and StandbyMode becomes true) can only occur if, at the transition point, either selfTestPass or guiResumeRequest is true. This is typically captured with a safety property that prevents forbidden transitions: it is always the case that if the system leaves SelfTestMode to StandbyMode, then either selfTestPass or guiResumeRequest holds at that moment. We formalize 'leave SelfTestMode and go to StandbyMode' as 'SelfTestMode & X(!SelfTestMode & StandbyMode)' and require it to imply '(selfTestPass | guiResumeRequest)'.

The requirement specifies that the system transitions from Self-Test Mode to VentilationOff Mode (called StandbyMode in the atomic propositions) when either (1) the self-test procedure is successfully completed (selfTestPass), or (2) the GUI requests resumption of ventilation (guiResumeRequest). Since the atomic propositions describe only the modes (SelfTestMode, StandbyMode), the successful self-test (selfTestPass), and the GUI request (guiResumeRequest), the correct LTL property should specify that whenever the system is in SelfTestMode, if either selfTestPass or guiResumeRequest becomes true, then eventually (F) the system reaches StandbyMode. In LTL, the '->' expresses the response relation, and the condition (selfTestPass | guiResumeRequest) expresses that either one can trigger the transition. This captures the intent of both subclauses, and is the most likely and direct translation. No previous LTL specification is available to compare, but common errors might be requiring both conditions (AND) or failing to require eventuality (F), resulting in no guarantee of transition. This LTL guarantees that, from SelfTestMode, whenever either trigger occurs, StandbyMode will eventually follow.

The requirement specifies the conditions under which a transition from SelfTestMode to StandbyMode (VentilationOff Mode) must occur. The two scenarios are (1) when the self-test procedure has completed successfully and (2) when the GUI issues a request to resume ventilation. These are described by atomic propositions 'selfTestPass' and 'guiResumeRequest', respectively. To capture the transition, we look for a change from 'SelfTestMode' being true to 'StandbyMode' being true, triggered by either of the two conditions. The LTL property asserts that globally, whenever the system is in 'SelfTestMode', if there comes a next step where either 'selfTestPass' or 'guiResumeRequest' holds, this is immediately followed by a transition to 'StandbyMode'. This translation captures both parts of the requirement and ensures the transition occurs as specified. The property avoids numerical comparison operators, respects the operator restrictions, and accurately encodes both sub-regulations.

The requirement specifies that a transition from Self-Test Mode to VentilationOff Mode (also referred to as Standby Mode) must occur in two situations: 1) when the self-test procedure has been completed successfully, 2) when the GUI asks for resuming ventilation. From the atomic propositions, 'SelfTestMode' indicates the system is in self-test, 'selfTestPass' indicates a successful self-test procedure, 'StandbyMode' is equivalent to VentilationOff, and 'guiResumeRequest' signals a GUI request to resume ventilation. The transition from SelfTestMode to StandbyMode should happen after either selfTestPass or guiResumeRequest. In LTL, this can be stated as: whenever the system is in SelfTestMode and either 'selfTestPass' or 'guiResumeRequest' becomes true, then eventually 'StandbyMode' must hold. This is formally captured by G(SelfTestMode & (selfTestPass | guiResumeRequest) -> F StandbyMode). This formulation guarantees that upon satisfying either of the two triggering conditions during Self-Test Mode, the system must, at some point afterwards, enter Standby (VentilationOff) Mode. Previous encodings that do not use the disjunction '(selfTestPass | guiResumeRequest)' or do not tie the trigger to when the system is in SelfTestMode would fail to precisely capture both required scenarios for transition.

The input specifies that the transition from Self-Test Mode to VentilationOff Mode (which is also called Standby Mode) occurs in two cases: (1) when the self-test procedure has successfully been completed, and (2) when the GUI asks for resuming ventilation. From the available atomic propositions, 'SelfTestMode' and 'StandbyMode' represent the two states in question, 'selfTestPass' indicates the successful completion of the self-test procedure, and 'guiResumeRequest' indicates the GUI asking to resume ventilation.

In LTL, we want to ensure that whenever we are in SelfTestMode, eventually, if 'selfTestPass' becomes true or 'guiResumeRequest' becomes true, then, next, the system transitions to StandbyMode (VentilationOff). This can be expressed as: 

G( SelfTestMode -> F( (selfTestPass | guiResumeRequest) & X StandbyMode ) )

This formula states that always, when the system is in SelfTestMode, it will eventually receive the condition for termination ('selfTestPass' or 'guiResumeRequest'), and immediately after, the system transitions to StandbyMode. This captures both subrequirements (CONT.4.1 and CONT.4.2). No previous LTL properties are given; thus, there is no need to explain why previous expressions may not capture the natural language.

The requirement states that the transition from Self-Test Mode to VentilationOff Mode (also known as Standby Mode) must occur under two alternative conditions: (1) when the self-test procedure has been successfully completed, or (2) when the GUI requests resuming ventilation. Since atomic propositions for the states are 'SelfTestMode' (for being in self-test), 'selfTestPass' (for successful self-test), and 'guiResumeRequest' (for GUI asking for resume), and 'StandbyMode' (for being in Standby/VentilationOff), the LTL translation should specify that whenever either (a) the self-test passes while the system is in SelfTestMode, or (b) the GUI requests resume while in SelfTestMode, it leads to being in StandbyMode. In LTL, this is best captured by defining that it is always the case (globally) that if the system is in SelfTestMode and either selfTestPass or guiResumeRequest becomes true, then eventually StandbyMode must hold. This correctly captures the requirement with no use of numerical comparisons and using only atomic propositions.

The requirement states that a transition from Self-Test Mode to VentilationOff Mode (which is the same as StandbyMode) should occur under either: (1) the self-test procedure has completed successfully, or (2) the GUI requests a resumption of ventilation. We interpret this in LTL as: whenever the system is in SelfTestMode, if either 'selfTestPass' or 'guiResumeRequest' occurs, then the system should (eventually, but immediately after or as soon as possible) leave SelfTestMode and enter StandbyMode. 

The property F((selfTestPass | guiResumeRequest) & SelfTestMode & StandbyMode) is incorrect, because it suggests that all should co-occur at some moment, which does not express the notion of transition. Instead, we require that 'if we are in SelfTestMode and either trigger occurs,' then 'eventually' StandbyMode is reached, and SelfTestMode is exited. Since LTL cannot directly represent actions or transitions, we model this by requiring that whenever in SelfTestMode and a relevant trigger happens, then eventually StandbyMode becomes true (i.e., the system enters VentilationOff).

Globally, whenever in SelfTestMode and (selfTestPass or guiResumeRequest) happen, it should eventually reach StandbyMode. This is best expressed as: G((SelfTestMode & (selfTestPass | guiResumeRequest)) -> F StandbyMode).

The requirement describes two independent ways for the system to transition from Self-Test Mode to VentilationOff Mode (Standby): 1) when the self-test procedure has successfully completed, and 2) when the GUI requests resuming ventilation. In the context of the available atomic propositions, 'SelfTestMode' indicates the system is in self-test, 'selfTestPass' the successful completion, 'guiResumeRequest' the GUI's request to resume, and 'StandbyMode' refers to VentilationOff/Standby. The specification should capture that whenever we are in SelfTestMode, if either 'selfTestPass' or 'guiResumeRequest' becomes true, then eventually the system will be in StandbyMode (VentilationOff). 

We use the LTL formula: G(SelfTestMode -> ((F selfTestPass | F guiResumeRequest) -> F StandbyMode))

This reads as: globally, while in SelfTestMode, if either eventually selfTestPass or eventually guiResumeRequest will be true, then eventually the system will reach StandbyMode. This ensures the transition happens upon either triggering event. (Note: This formalization presumes that the transition's effect is observable, i.e., the system enters StandbyMode. If previous translations simply required something like F StandbyMode after either event without reference to SelfTestMode context, they would not be correct because they might allow transitions at unrelated times.)
output_LTL: G(SelfTestMode -> ((F selfTestPass | F guiResumeRequest) -> F StandbyMode))

explanation:
The requirement states that a transition from Self-Test Mode to Standby Mode (which is described as equivalent to VentilationOff Mode) is allowed under two (OR-ed) conditions: (1) when the self-test procedure has successfully completed, and (2) when the GUI requests resumption of ventilation. In terms of atomic propositions, 'SelfTestMode' represents being in self-test mode, 'StandbyMode' represents being in (ventilation off) standby mode, 'selfTestPass' means the test completed successfully, and 'guiResumeRequest' indicates the user's request to resume. To encode a mode transition in LTL, we express that: whenever we are in SelfTestMode and (in the next moment) we are in StandbyMode, this only happens if either the self-test passed or the GUI requested ventilation. (Note: we require that the only way to leave SelfTestMode for StandbyMode is via one of those two conditions.)

LTL formula: Globally, if the system is in SelfTestMode now and in StandbyMode at the next time step, then either selfTestPass or guiResumeRequest holds at this time step.

This formulation directly captures the described transition constraints and does not rely on state variable comparison or other disallowed operators. Alternative LTLs might miss that the transition should only occur under these specific circumstances, but by explicitly checking the change and the associated triggers, this property enforces the requirement.

The natural language specification describes a required transition from Self-Test Mode to VentilationOff (Standby) Mode that should occur (1) when the self-test procedure completes successfully and (2) when the GUI requests to resume ventilation. The available atomic propositions are 'SelfTestMode' (indicates the system is in SelfTest Mode), 'StandbyMode' (indicates the system is in VentilationOff/Standby Mode), 'selfTestPass' (self-test completed successfully), and 'guiResumeRequest' (the GUI asks for resuming ventilation). Neither mode transitions nor explicit transition actions are directly represented as atomic propositions, so the best possible LTL expresses that whenever the system is in SelfTestMode, eventually, if either 'selfTestPass' or 'guiResumeRequest' becomes true, the system must transition to StandbyMode (i.e., leave SelfTestMode and enter StandbyMode). This is expressed with an implication: when in SelfTestMode, if 'selfTestPass' or 'guiResumeRequest' become true, then eventually the system will be in StandbyMode. Note: This does not guarantee immediate transition, but captures eventuality given the atomic propositions. Prior LTL properties using only 'selfTestPass -> F StandbyMode' would not capture both triggers ('guiResumeRequest' and 'selfTestPass') as specified in the input. Including both covers the requirement more adequately.
